##### Full Extract (verbatim with line numbers)
```text
L0001 | # PROJECT_LAW
L0002 | ## Canonical Project Law for Monorepo & Agentic Systems
L0003 | 
L0004 | **Status:** TIER-0 AUTHORITY  
L0005 | **Scope:** All projects, agents, specs, tools, CI, and contributors  
L0006 | **Precedence:** Overrides all other documents unless explicitly stated  
L0007 | **Change Control:** Append-only via ADR; breaking changes allowed but must be explicit
L0008 | 
L0009 | ---
L0010 | 
L0011 | ## PREAMBLE — WHAT THIS DOCUMENT IS
L0012 | 
L0013 | This document defines the **constitutional law** of the project.
L0014 | 
L0015 | It governs:
L0016 | - **How work is allowed to happen**
L0017 | - **How agents behave**
L0018 | - **How projects are structured**
L0019 | - **How systems evolve over time**
L0020 | 
L0021 | If any output, code, plan, or agent action conflicts with this document,  
L0022 | **this document wins**.
L0023 | 
L0024 | This file canonicalizes and supersedes the following source frameworks:
L0025 | - Guardrails (anti-entropy, anti-silent compromise)
L0026 | - Project Organization Law (PRD-first, command-ified work)
L0027 | - Meta-Orchestrated Spec-Driven Agentic Framework
L0028 | 
L0029 | ---
L0030 | 
L0031 | ## DEFINITIONS (GLOBAL)
L0032 | 
L0033 | - **HARD** — mandatory; violation invalidates output
L0034 | - **SOFT** — default expectation; deviation must be justified
L0035 | - **OPTIONAL** — allowed, not required
L0036 | - **Agent** — any automated or semi-automated actor
L0037 | - **Human** — any contributor or operator
L0038 | - **Spec** — a document that defines intent before execution
L0039 | - **SOT** — single source of truth for a system
L0040 | - **ADR** — architecture decision record
L0041 | 
L0042 | ---
L0043 | 
L0044 | ## ARTICLE I — GUARDRAILS (LAW-GRD)
L0045 | 
L0046 | ### LAW-GRD-001 (HARD) — No Silent Assumptions
L0047 | No agent or human may assume requirements, intent, constraints, or permissions.  
L0048 | All assumptions must be explicitly stated or derived from a spec.
L0049 | 
L0050 | ### LAW-GRD-002 (HARD) — No Silent State Mutation
L0051 | No system may mutate state, learn, summarize, or optimize without producing an explicit artifact or record.
L0052 | 
L0053 | ### LAW-GRD-003 (HARD) — No Backwards Compatibility by Default
L0054 | Breaking changes are allowed by default if they improve clarity or correctness.  
L0055 | Backwards compatibility must be explicitly justified.
L0056 | 
L0057 | ### LAW-GRD-004 (HARD) — Plan ≠ Execute
L0058 | Planning, reasoning, and execution are separate phases.  
L0059 | No execution may occur inside planning, exploration, discussion, or UI drafting.
L0060 | 
L0061 | ### LAW-GRD-005 (HARD) — No “Just Make It Work”
L0062 | Temporary hacks are forbidden unless explicitly labeled, scoped, tracked, and scheduled for removal.
L0063 | 
L0064 | ---
L0065 | 
L0066 | ## ARTICLE II — PROJECT ORGANIZATION LAW (LAW-ORG)
L0067 | 
L0068 | ### LAW-ORG-001 (HARD) — PRD-First Development
L0069 | Every meaningful unit of work must begin with a spec or PRD.  
L0070 | Code without a governing spec is invalid.
L0071 | 
L0072 | ### LAW-ORG-002 (HARD) — Command-ify Everything
L0073 | All work must be reducible to explicit commands: inputs, outputs, side effects.  
L0074 | If it cannot be commanded, it is not ready to execute.
L0075 | 
L0076 | ### LAW-ORG-003 (HARD) — Context Reset Discipline
L0077 | Context must be discoverable, reloadable, reconstructible.  
L0078 | No work may rely on implicit conversational memory.
L0079 | 
L0080 | ### LAW-ORG-004 (SOFT) — Modular Rules Architecture
L0081 | Rules/constraints should be modular, composable, independently testable.
L0082 | 
L0083 | ### LAW-ORG-005 (SOFT) — System Evolution Mindset
L0084 | Failures must produce rules/tests/specs, not tribal knowledge.
L0085 | 
L0086 | ---
L0087 | 
L0088 | ## ARTICLE III — META-ORCHESTRATED AGENTIC FRAMEWORK (LAW-META)
L0089 | 
L0090 | ### LAW-META-001 (HARD) — Baseline + Deltas Model
L0091 | There must exist a Baseline and explicit Deltas. No mutation of baseline without a delta.
L0092 | 
L0093 | ### LAW-META-002 (HARD) — Single Source of Truth
L0094 | Every system must declare exactly one SOT. Duplicated truth is a defect.
L0095 | 
L0096 | ### LAW-META-003 (HARD) — Role-Bound Agents
L0097 | Agents operate in explicit roles. No silent role-crossing.
L0098 | 
L0099 | ### LAW-META-004 (HARD) — Deterministic Loop
L0100 | All workflows follow: `Research → Context → Plan → Act → Report`. Skips require justification.
L0101 | 
L0102 | ### LAW-META-005 (HARD) — Meta-Learning Is Structural
L0103 | Learning may produce specs/rules/tests. It may not silently alter runtime behavior.
L0104 | 
L0105 | ---
L0106 | 
L0107 | ## ARTICLE IV — ENFORCEMENT & AUTOMATION (LAW-ENF)
L0108 | 
L0109 | ### LAW-ENF-001 (HARD) — Mandatory Load Order
L0110 | Agents and tools must load:
L0111 | 1. `PROJECT_LAW.md`
L0112 | 2. `SOT.md`
L0113 | 3. Relevant specs
L0114 | 
L0115 | No execution before load.
L0116 | 
L0117 | ### LAW-ENF-002 (HARD) — Auditability
L0118 | All actions must be attributable, reproducible, explainable. If it cannot be audited, it cannot run.
L0119 | 
L0120 | ### LAW-ENF-003 (SOFT) — CI & Gatekeeping
L0121 | Enforcement should be automated via lint/spec checks/acceptance tests where possible.
L0122 | 
L0123 | ---
L0124 | 
L0125 | ## ARTICLE V — CHANGE CONTROL (LAW-CHG)
L0126 | 
L0127 | ### LAW-CHG-001 (HARD) — Modifying Project Law
L0128 | Changes require an ADR, explicit rationale, and version increment.
L0129 | 
L0130 | ### LAW-CHG-002 (HARD) — Append-Only Mentality
L0131 | Rewrites must preserve history, intent, and traceability.
L0132 | 
L0133 | ---
L0134 | 
L0135 | ## OPERATIONAL CHEAT SHEET (NON-NORMATIVE)
L0136 | 
L0137 | - Writing code → LAW-ORG-001..003
L0138 | - Building agents → LAW-META-001..005
L0139 | - Designing workflows → LAW-GRD-004
L0140 | - Refactoring → LAW-GRD-003
L0141 | - Automating decisions → LAW-ENF-001..002
L0142 | 
L0143 | ---
L0144 | 
L0145 | ## FINAL CLAUSE
L0146 | 
L0147 | This law protects clarity, enables speed, and prevents entropy.
```

##### Full Extract (verbatim with line numbers)
```text
L0001 | 
L0002 | # APPENDIX A — PROJECT ORGANIZATION LAW
L0003 | ## Agentic Engineering Techniques (Formalized)
L0004 | 
L0005 | Status: Append-only  
L0006 | Scope: Project organization, execution hygiene, agent alignment  
L0007 | Compatibility: Fully compliant with Baseline + Deltas + SOT enforcement
L0008 | 
L0009 | This appendix operationalizes five agentic engineering techniques as non-negotiable system law.
L0010 | They are organizational constraints, not advice.
L0011 | 
L0012 | ---
L0013 | 
L0014 | ## A1. PRD-FIRST DEVELOPMENT (INTENT ANCHOR)
L0015 | 
L0016 | Law:
L0017 | No execution may occur without a canonical intent document.
L0018 | 
L0019 | Framework Binding:
L0020 | PRDs are part of the Baseline truth layer.
L0021 | 
L0022 | Required Location:
L0023 | /spec/Baseline/PRD.md (or embedded in Vision + Requirements)
L0024 | 
L0025 | Enforcement:
L0026 | - All tasks must reference PRD sections.
L0027 | - Brownfield projects must maintain a reverse-PRD.
L0028 | 
L0029 | ---
L0030 | 
L0031 | ## A2. MODULAR RULES ARCHITECTURE (CONTEXT ISOLATION)
L0032 | 
L0033 | Law:
L0034 | Context must be explicitly loaded, never ambient.
L0035 | 
L0036 | Framework Binding:
L0037 | Rules live in /agent/* and /references/* and are loaded on demand.
L0038 | 
L0039 | Required Structure:
L0040 | /agent/POLICY.md  
L0041 | /references/api.md  
L0042 | /references/components.md  
L0043 | /references/auth.md  
L0044 | /references/deploy.md  
L0045 | 
L0046 | Enforcement:
L0047 | - Agents must declare loaded context.
L0048 | - Irrelevant context halts execution.
L0049 | 
L0050 | ---
L0051 | 
L0052 | ## A3. COMMAND-IFY EVERYTHING (DETERMINISM LAYER)
L0053 | 
L0054 | Law:
L0055 | Repeated actions become commands or workflows.
L0056 | 
L0057 | Framework Binding:
L0058 | Commands are execution tools, not reasoning skills.
L0059 | 
L0060 | Required Location:
L0061 | /tools/commands.md  
L0062 | /tools/workflows.md  
L0063 | 
L0064 | Enforcement:
L0065 | - Instructions repeated twice must be formalized.
L0066 | - Commands are deterministic.
L0067 | 
L0068 | ---
L0069 | 
L0070 | ## A4. CONTEXT RESET (SESSION BOUNDARY LAW)
L0071 | 
L0072 | Law:
L0073 | Planning and execution must not share degraded context.
L0074 | 
L0075 | Framework Binding:
L0076 | Enforced through session orchestration.
L0077 | 
L0078 | Mandatory Flow:
L0079 | Research → Build Context → Plan → DOC → RESET → EXEC → Report
L0080 | 
L0081 | Enforcement:
L0082 | - Execution agents only consume written artifacts.
L0083 | 
L0084 | ---
L0085 | 
L0086 | ## A5. SYSTEM EVOLUTION MINDSET (COMPOUNDING LAW)
L0087 | 
L0088 | Law:
L0089 | Every failure must upgrade the system.
L0090 | 
L0091 | Framework Binding:
L0092 | Handled by the Meta Agent.
L0093 | 
L0094 | Required Outcome:
L0095 | One of the following must be updated:
L0096 | - /agent/*
L0097 | - /references/*
L0098 | - /tools/*
L0099 | - /spec/Deltas/*
L0100 | - Acceptance tests
L0101 | 
L0102 | ---
L0103 | 
L0104 | ## A6. UNIFIED INVARIANT
L0105 | 
L0106 | Agents operate inside the system.  
L0107 | The system evolves.  
L0108 | Memory is written, not remembered.
L0109 | 
L0110 | ---
L0111 | 
L0112 | ## A7. ENFORCEMENT SUMMARY
L0113 | 
L0114 | Execution halts if:
L0115 | - PRD missing
L0116 | - Context not declared
L0117 | - Commands repeated manually
L0118 | - No context reset
L0119 | - Failures not converted into system updates
L0120 | 
L0121 | ---
L0122 | 
L0123 | End of Appendix A
```

L0001 | ---
L0002 | title: "A2rchitech Session Summary — Onboarding & Login Fun (Login Arcade)"
L0003 | date: "2026-01-26"
L0004 | topic: "onboarding-login-fun"
L0005 | scope: ["auth", "onboarding", "ui", "game", "canvas"]

L0008 | 
L0009 | # Purpose
L0010 | Add a lightweight, 2D, Sonic-like microgame to the **Sign In / Register** screens to reduce friction and increase delight, while keeping authentication fully usable.
L0011 | 
L0012 | # Visual direction (concepts)
L0013 | 
L0014 | ## Concept v1 — Full scene
L0015 | ![Concept v1](./concept_v1_full_scene.png)
L0016 | 
L0017 | ## Concept v2 — Simplified
L0018 | ![Concept v2](./concept_v2_simplified_light.png)
L0019 | 
L0020 | ## Concept v3 — Dark + logo depth (target)
L0021 | ![Concept v3](./concept_v3_dark.png)
L0022 | 
L0023 | # Product constraints
L0024 | - Auth form always usable.
L0025 | - Ambient attract mode by default.
L0026 | - One-input control.
L0027 | - Mobile-safe performance.
L0028 | - Dark background, logo-only scenery with parallax depth.
L0029 | 
L0030 | # Login Arcade rotation
L0031 | Random microgame chosen on refresh using seeded RNG.
L0032 | 
L0033 | Rotation modes:
L0034 | - refreshRandom
L0035 | - sessionSticky (default)
L0036 | - daily
L0037 | 
L0038 | # Microgame pool
L0039 | Runner Jump, Jetpack Hover, Shield Timing, Wave Dodge, Rhythm Gates, Grapple Swing,
L0040 | Lane Switch Runner, Pong Solo, Asteroid Drift, Stack Balance.
L0041 | 
L0042 | # Depth tricks
L0043 | Parallax layers, fog/vignette, glow halo, scale-speed coupling.
L0044 | 
L0045 | # Build blueprint
L0046 | Single engine + swappable modes.
L0047 | 
L0048 | Modules export:
L0049 | id, title, weight, init, update, render, teardown.
L0050 | 
L0051 | File layout:
L0052 | components/LoginArcade.tsx
L0053 | game/engine.ts
L0054 | game/modes/*
L0055 | game/modeRegistry.ts
L0056 | game/modePicker.ts
L0057 | 
L0058 | v1 modes:
L0059 | runner_jump
L0060 | jetpack_hover
L0061 | shield_timing
```

##### Full Extract (verbatim with line numbers)
```text
L0001 | # A2rchitech Session Summary — AgentOps Extension (A2R)
L0002 | **Date:** 2026-01-26
L0003 | **Topic:** AgentOps extension; Browser tab + dynamic miniapp capsules; remote-browser transport; CopilotKit/AG-UI/A2A integration; web agents.
L0004 | 
L0005 | ---
L0006 | 
L0007 | ## 1) UI IA Decision (keep tabs minimal)
L0008 | **Keep existing essential tabs:**
L0009 | - Chat
L0010 | - Console
L0011 | - Studio (multimodal / ComfyUI / agentic builders)
L0012 | - Registry
L0013 | - Marketplace
L0014 | - Browser (in development)
L0015 | 
L0016 | **No extra “Mission Control” tab list.**  
L0017 | Any additional complexity must live as **toggle modes within existing tabs**, especially Console and Browser.
L0018 | 
L0019 | ---
L0020 | 
L0021 | ## 2) Console as AgentOps Surface (modes inside Console)
L0022 | Console is not “just logs”; it is the operational surface for agents + tools + workflows with internal modes:
L0023 | - **Observe:** event/log/trace view (filtered by context)
L0024 | - **Command:** terminal + command palette + quick actions (spawn/bind/run)
L0025 | - **Visual:** abstract/RTS-like visualization of agent actions (optional)
L0026 | 
L0027 | **Rule:** One underlying state/event spine; multiple renderers.
L0028 | 
L0029 | ---
L0030 | 
L0031 | ## 3) Chat Tab Direction
L0032 | Chat should remain **human-first** (conversation surface), not overloaded with separate “Direct/Delegate/Review” modes as a primary concept.
L0033 | Instead: **context binding** (chat target pill) such as:
L0034 | - Human → System
L0035 | - Human → Agent
L0036 | - Human → Workflow Run
L0037 | This implies direct vs review vs delegate without adding visible mode clutter.
L0038 | 
L0039 | ---
L0040 | 
L0041 | ## 4) Studio Deep Dive (agentic builders + multimodal)
L0042 | Studio is for **authoring** (not live operations):
L0043 | - **Agent Builder** (definitions, permissions, bindings)
L0044 | - **Workflow Builder** (DAG, versions, simulate/dry-run)
L0045 | - **Multimodal/ComfyUI pipelines** (graphs that produce artifacts + reusable pipeline defs)
L0046 | - **Artifacts & Templates** (outputs + reusable defs)
L0047 | 
L0048 | Studio outputs versioned definitions → **Registry**; execution/monitoring happens in **Console**.
L0049 | 
L0050 | ---
L0051 | 
L0052 | ## 5) End-to-End Flow (Browser → Studio → Registry → Console)
L0053 | 1. **Browser**: browse/search; capture page snapshots/clips; cite sources
L0054 | 2. **Studio**: convert artifacts into agents/workflows/pipelines
L0055 | 3. **Registry**: publish versioned definitions + permissions
L0056 | 4. **Console**: run workflows; observe/command/visualize; record runs + artifacts
L0057 | 
L0058 | ---
L0059 | 
L0060 | ## 6) Browser Tab Goals (miniapp capsules + gentabs-like clustering)
L0061 | Browser must be a **real browsing surface** while supporting:
L0062 | - **Clustered miniapp capsules** (Option A): “project/task clusters” that group pages + tools + agents
L0063 | - **Overlay miniapps** (Option B): contextual cards on top of current page
L0064 | 
L0065 | **Decision leaning:** Option A (clustered) as primary organization, with Option B overlays as secondary behavior.
L0066 | 
L0067 | **Mockups produced:**
L0068 | - browser_mockup_option_A.png
L0069 | - browser_mockup_option_B.png
L0070 | 
L0071 | ---
L0072 | 
L0073 | ## 7) Core Problem: web-only browser not loading pages
L0074 | If current approach is iframe/fetch-render, many sites won’t load due to CSP/X-Frame-Options and cross-origin constraints.
L0075 | A “real browser engine” must render pages, either:
L0076 | - embedded native webview (Electron/Tauri), or
L0077 | - remote browser service streamed to the web app.
L0078 | 
L0079 | ---
L0080 | 
L0081 | ## 8) Remote Browser Transport Options (wrapper approach)
L0082 | **Option 1:** screenshot-per-action (fast, but choppy)  
L0083 | **Option 2:** WebSocket frame streaming (10–15 FPS; simpler than WebRTC)  
L0084 | **Option 3:** WebRTC streaming (best UX; most complex ops)
L0085 | 
L0086 | **User preference:** Option 3 (WebRTC) long-term.
L0087 | 
L0088 | **Recommendation for de-risking:** implement **Option 2 first** on macOS (local service), then upgrade transport to WebRTC once stable.
L0089 | 
L0090 | ---
L0091 | 
L0092 | ## 9) CopilotKit, but proprietary (no Copilot branding)
L0093 | Three levels:
L0094 | - **A) Use core/runtime only** and build your own UI (no Copilot UI components) — best balance.
L0095 | - **B) Fork/vendor CopilotKit** into internal packages (white-label, more maintenance).
L0096 | - **C) Skip CopilotKit** if AG-UI/A2UI already covers requirements.
L0097 | 
L0098 | ---
L0099 | 
L0100 | ## 10) WebVM Consideration (Linux WASM VM in repo)
L0101 | Potential simplification if WebVM provides a **usable GUI framebuffer** that can be drawn to canvas at practical FPS.
L0102 | If WebVM is headless-only, it won’t replace the remote browser surface for interactive browsing.
L0103 | 
L0104 | Decision hinge:
L0105 | - A) WebVM exposes interactive GUI framebuffer → WebVM-first plan viable
L0106 | - B) Headless/CLI only → proceed with local browser-session-service streaming
L0107 | 
L0108 | ---
L0109 | 
L0110 | ## 11) Required Agent Types for Browser
L0111 | All agents operate on the same **browser sessionId**:
L0112 | - **Text Web Agent:** extract readable text/links/citations (server-side extraction)
L0113 | - **Vision Web Agent:** interpret snapshots/keyframes for what’s actually rendered
L0114 | - **Computer-Use Agent:** UI automation (UI-TARS widget already present in repo)
L0115 | 
L0116 | ---
L0117 | 
L0118 | ## 12) Planned Services (implementation gameplan)
L0119 | Create:
L0120 | - `services/browser-session-service/` (Playwright + streaming + input replay)
L0121 | - `services/agui-gateway/` (AG-UI routing to capsules/sessions)
L0122 | - `services/copilot-runtime/` (self-hosted CopilotKit runtime)
L0123 | - `services/a2a-gateway/` (Agent2Agent discovery/tasks/artifacts bridging)
L0124 | 
L0125 | Frontend:

##### Full Extract (verbatim with line numbers)
```text
L0001 | # A2rchitech Session Summary — CLI Agents as a Service + Skill Registry Augmentation
L0002 | **Date:** 2026-01-26 (America/Chicago)  
L0003 | **Scope:** A2rchitech platform architecture direction — running CLI agents as a service, abstracting CLI tools as agent skills, and augmenting the existing Skills Registry with a browser-operator CLI (`vercel-labs/agent-browser`).
L0004 | 
L0005 | ---
L0006 | 
L0007 | ## 1) Objective
L0008 | Establish a production-grade pattern where:
L0009 | - Agents can run as **services** (job-based runners) rather than interactive, one-off terminal sessions.
L0010 | - The platform treats downloaded/installed CLI tools (and MCP-exposed tools) as **typed skills**.
L0011 | - The LLM remains a **planner**; execution happens in a **policy-gated runner**.
L0012 | - Browser automation is added as a first-class skill via a CLI operator, to augment development workflows (UI verification, scripted repros, evidence capture).
L0013 | 
L0014 | ---
L0015 | 
L0016 | ## 2) Definitions & Mental Models
L0017 | 
L0018 | ### 2.1 “CLI agent as a service”
L0019 | A CLI agent becomes infrastructure:
L0020 | - Always-on runner (daemon/container/server)
L0021 | - Accepts jobs via API / queue / cron
L0022 | - Executes tool-based workflows
L0023 | - Produces structured results + artifacts + logs/traces
L0024 | - Enforces policy constraints (allowed tools, sandbox, network, secrets)
L0025 | 
L0026 | **Core benefit:** decouple agent planning from execution environment; enable repeatability, isolation, and scale.
L0027 | 
L0028 | ### 2.2 “Skills = invocable programs with contracts”
L0029 | A skill is any capability that can be invoked reliably:
L0030 | - CLI tools already provide stable interfaces (args/flags, exit codes).
L0031 | - Wrap each tool behind a **Skill Contract**:
L0032 |   - `name`
L0033 |   - invocation template
L0034 |   - input schema
L0035 |   - output schema (normalized, ideally JSON)
L0036 |   - safety class (read-only / write / network / privileged)
L0037 |   - timeouts + resource limits
L0038 |   - sandbox requirements
L0039 | 
L0040 | **Agent never constructs raw shell strings.** It calls named skills with typed args.
L0041 | 
L0042 | ### 2.3 Bash tool as universal adapter (gated)
L0043 | Bash remains useful only as a controlled adapter layer:
L0044 | - Allowlist binaries
L0045 | - Structured arg passing only
L0046 | - Sandbox isolation (container/microVM)
L0047 | - Default deny network
L0048 | - Capture stdout/stderr/exit codes
L0049 | - Normalize outputs to structured formats
L0050 | 
L0051 | ---
L0052 | 
L0053 | ## 3) Architecture: Control Plane vs Data Plane
L0054 | 
L0055 | ### 3.1 Control plane (LLM)
L0056 | - Produces a structured plan referencing skill names + parameters
L0057 | - Does NOT carry the full tool registry in its context window
L0058 | 
L0059 | ### 3.2 Data plane (Runner)
L0060 | - Resolves skill calls
L0061 | - Validates policy
L0062 | - Executes in sandbox
L0063 | - Returns typed outputs + artifacts
L0064 | - Records full audit trail (tool calls, logs, timestamps)
L0065 | 
L0066 | **Key principle agreed:** tool discovery + invocation should be **out-of-band** (runner-side), not token-side (prompt).
L0067 | 
L0068 | ---
L0069 | 
L0070 | ## 4) MCP & “No MCP in context window”
L0071 | Session conclusion:
L0072 | - It is desirable to keep the tool directory out of the context window.
L0073 | - Even without embedding MCP catalogs in the prompt, a runner can:
L0074 |   - discover tools/servers
L0075 |   - invoke them
L0076 |   - return results
L0077 | - This produces smaller prompts, fewer hallucinated tools, and safer enforcement.
L0078 | 
L0079 | **Interpretation:** MCP becomes an execution fabric; the prompt carries stable skill names, not full capability docs.
L0080 | 
L0081 | ---
L0082 | 
L0083 | ## 5) Augmentation via `vercel-labs/agent-browser`
L0084 | 
L0085 | ### 5.1 Why it matters
L0086 | `agent-browser` is a browser operation CLI designed to be invoked as a discrete tool. This fits the skills approach:
L0087 | - A single “browser operator” skill replaces ad-hoc Playwright scripts.
L0088 | - It provides an agent-friendly workflow:
L0089 |   - `snapshot` returns an accessibility-tree with element refs
L0090 |   - actions reference those refs (click/fill/get-text/screenshot)
L0091 | - This produces a stable intermediate representation the agent can reason about.
L0092 | 
L0093 | ### 5.2 What we planned to build
L0094 | Integrate `agent-browser` into the existing Skills Registry as a first-class, stateful skill:
L0095 | 
L0096 | **Skill family:** `browser.operator`
L0097 | 
L0098 | **Session-based interface (wrapper-level):**
L0099 | - `browser.open(url) -> {session_id}`
L0100 | - `browser.snapshot(session_id) -> {tree, refs}`
L0101 | - `browser.click(session_id, ref)`
L0102 | - `browser.fill(session_id, ref, value)`
L0103 | - `browser.get_text(session_id, ref) -> {text}`
L0104 | - `browser.screenshot(session_id) -> {artifact_path}`
L0105 | - `browser.close(session_id)`
L0106 | 
L0107 | **Wrapper responsibilities:**
L0108 | - session lifecycle management
L0109 | - normalize outputs into canonical JSON
L0110 | - store screenshots as artifacts
L0111 | - enforce domain/network policy gates
L0112 | - standardize error handling (timeouts, exit codes)
L0113 | 
L0114 | ---
L0115 | 
L0116 | ## 6) Immediate Implementation Plan (Minimal Path)
L0117 | Deliver the smallest integration that compounds:
L0118 | 
L0119 | 1. Add registry entry for `browser.operator` mapping to `agent-browser` executable.
L0120 | 2. Ensure `agent-browser` is installed in the sandbox image/environment used by the runner.
L0121 | 3. Implement an adapter that turns `snapshot` output into a canonical JSON structure with refs.
L0122 | 4. Implement wrapper session abstraction (`session_id`) even if the CLI is inherently stateless; store per-session profile/temp dir mappings.
L0123 | 5. Wire into the runner’s skill resolver and policy engine.
L0124 | 6. Enable artifact capture (screenshots) and log/tracing for each tool call.
L0125 | 
L0126 | ---
L0127 | 
L0128 | ## 7) Intended Dev Workflow Benefits
L0129 | Once integrated, agents can:
L0130 | 
L0131 | - **UI verification**: open dev URL → snapshot → assert controls exist → click through flows → capture screenshots.
L0132 | - **Repro evidence**: convert “steps to reproduce” into an executable plan with supporting artifacts.
L0133 | - **Reduce context switching**: browsing and verification become callable skills with auditable outputs.
L0134 | 
L0135 | ---
L0136 | 
L0137 | ## 8) Non-negotiables / Constraints
L0138 | - No free-form shell access in production path.
L0139 | - Skill calls must be typed and policy-gated.
L0140 | - Prefer deterministic outputs (JSON modes, adapters/parsers).
L0141 | - Pin tool versions in images to avoid output drift.
L0142 | - Default deny network; allowlists for browser domains where required.
L0143 | - Full observability: store prompts/plans, tool calls, stdout/stderr, exit codes, artifacts.
L0144 | 
L0145 | ---
L0146 | 
L0147 | ## 9) Deliverable Produced In-Session
L0148 | A paste-ready implementation Markdown spec was produced (the “Implementation Specification v1.0”) describing:
L0149 | - CLI agent service model
L0150 | - skills contracts
L0151 | - gated bash executor
L0152 | - out-of-band tool resolution
L0153 | - `browser.operator` session-based wrapper interface
L0154 | - minimal integration steps
L0155 | - security + observability requirements
L0156 | 
L0157 | (If needed, merge this summary with that spec as a single canonical work item.)
L0158 | 
L0159 | ---
```

L0052 | ## 1) Operating lens used for all analysis

L0054 | - **Agent Harness**: long-running, multi-agent execution environment with human-in-loop gates.

L0057 | - **Capsules & UI Runtime**: modular mini-apps (“capsules”), including HUMAN vs AGENT renderers, inspector tooling, and stage/bounds correctness.
L0058 | - **Memory & Replay**: checkpoints, artifacts, extraction pipelines, time-travel/replay, audit logs.

```text
L0001 | ---
L0002 | topic: "gizzi-avatar-3d integration"
L0003 | date: 2026-01-26

L0010 | ---
L0011 | 
L0012 | # A2rchitech Session Summary — Gizzi Avatar (avatar-3d)
L0013 | 
L0014 | ## Context
L0015 | This session focused on whether the GitHub repo `0xGF/avatar-3d` can be used to build **Gizzi’s 3D avatar** inside the **A2rchitech productivity OS / agentic platform**.
L0016 | 
L0017 | - Proposed input repo: `avatar-3d` (Three.js-based 3D avatar rendering / control in browser)  
L0018 | - Target platform: A2rchitech (Electron shell + capsule system; HUMAN vs AGENT renderer separation)
L0019 | 
L0020 | ## Decisions and Positioning
L0021 | ### 1) Role of `avatar-3d` in A2rchitech
L0022 | **Use it as a presentation-layer primitive only**:
L0023 | - The repo is suitable as a **browser-safe 3D humanoid renderer** (Three.js + glTF/rigged avatar pipeline).
L0024 | - It is *not* an “avatar system” for an agent by itself (no cognition, state model, speech sync, identity, or orchestration).
L0025 | - Therefore it should be integrated as a **Capsule** that subscribes to agent state, never generating agent decisions.
L0026 | 
L0027 | **Architecture principle**: *Avatar renders; agent thinks.*
L0028 | 
L0029 | ### 2) Boundary rule: no feedback loop
L0030 | The avatar must not “think” or influence agent reasoning directly. It should be a **pure subscriber** to an intent/state bus.
L0031 | 
L0032 | - Agent Core emits: intent + speech timing + status
L0033 | - Avatar Capsule renders: animation, pose, visemes, idle/thinking/speaking states
L0034 | - The avatar never becomes a control plane.
L0035 | 
L0036 | This preserves deterministic orchestration and avoids recursion / coupling failures.
L0037 | 
L0038 | ## Proposed Integration Design
L0039 | ### Placement in repo
L0040 | Suggested structure (capsule-first):
L0041 | ```
L0042 | /apps/shell
L0043 |   /capsules
L0044 |     /gizzi-avatar
L0045 |       AvatarCanvas.tsx
L0046 |       AvatarController.ts
L0047 |       assets/ (gltf/vrm, animations)
L0048 |       README.md
L0049 |       contract/
L0050 |         avatar.intent.schema.json
L0051 | ```
L0052 | 
L0053 | ### Runtime mode
L0054 | - **Runs under HUMAN renderer** (Electron BrowserView / UI surface).
L0055 | - Subscribes to agent events; does not execute agent tools.
L0056 | - Must be isolated from AGENT Playwright automation surface to avoid tool confusion and stage bounds/render issues.
L0057 | 
L0058 | ### Control Contract (Intent Bus)
L0059 | Minimum intent types (example set):
L0060 | - idle
L0061 | - thinking
L0062 | - listening
L0063 | - speaking (with intensity + timing hooks)
L0064 | - ack
L0065 | - error
L0066 | 
L0067 | This becomes a formal JSON schema so other components (voice engine, agent state machine) can target it consistently.
L0068 | 
L0069 | ## Gaps (Non-Optional Work Still Required)
L0070 | `avatar-3d` alone does **not** provide:
L0071 | 
L0072 | 1. **Speech sync (visemes / phonemes)**
L0073 |    - Need mapping from TTS output timing → mouth shapes (viseme blendshapes) or bone-based jaw/mouth rigs.
L0074 |    - Requires a TTS engine that can output word/phoneme timestamps, or an approximation layer.
L0075 | 
L0076 | 2. **Emotion/affect abstraction**
L0077 |    - Must define a symbolic affect state (e.g., calm/confident/uncertain/alert) and map it to animation blends.
L0078 |    - Avoid “emotion = facial expression” naive mapping. Keep it controlled and minimal.
L0079 | 
L0080 | 3. **Performance gating**
L0081 |    - FPS throttling, idle sleep, reduced update loops when minimized, GPU fallback.
L0082 |    - Deterministic bounds and DPR handling inside Electron (important in A2rchitech due to windowed capsule system).
L0083 | 
L0084 | 4. **Avatar identity spec**
L0085 |    - Gizzi should be stylized with consistent geometry, palette, motion constraints.
L0086 |    - Decide: GLTF-only vs VRM-only pipeline (VRM often simplifies humanoid + visemes, but adds constraints).
L0087 | 
L0088 | ## A2rchitech Mapping
L0089 | ### A2rchitech Tier / Layer Mapping
L0090 | - **UI / Presentation Layer**: avatar-3d (Three.js renderer) lives here.
L0091 | - **Capsule Layer**: `gizzi-avatar` capsule encapsulates rendering, assets, controller.
L0092 | - **State/Intent Bus**: subscribes to agent state emitted by Brain Runtime (or equivalent).
L0093 | - **Agent Core**: emits intent; does not import avatar logic.
L0094 | - **Voice Layer**: provides TTS + timestamp info to drive speaking animation.
L0095 | 
L0096 | ### Law Layer / Guardrails Alignment (implicit)
L0097 | - Treat avatar capsule as **read-only UI** with a strict contract.
L0098 | - Changes to contract go through /spec Deltas and acceptance tests (if you choose to formalize).
L0099 | - Avoid “UI thinking” and prevent cross-layer coupling.
L0100 | 
L0101 | ## Action Plan (Concrete Next Steps)
L0102 | 1. **Fork** `0xGF/avatar-3d` into A2rchitech-owned namespace (or vendor it as a submodule).
L0103 | 2. Build `capsules/gizzi-avatar` wrapper:
L0104 |    - Minimal render loop
L0105 |    - Asset loading
L0106 |    - Animation controller
L0107 | 3. Define and commit `avatar.intent.schema.json` (contract for intent events).
L0108 | 4. Integrate with A2rchitech event bus:
L0109 |    - Subscribe to `agent_state` / `speech_state` events
L0110 |    - Map to avatar intents
L0111 | 5. Decide avatar asset pipeline:
L0112 |    - Pick rig format (VRM recommended if visemes are a priority; GLTF acceptable if you own the rig + blendshapes)
L0113 | 6. Add performance + Electron considerations:
L0114 |    - DPR normalization
L0115 |    - Resize handling
L0116 |    - Idle sleep when hidden/minimized
L0117 | 7. Add acceptance checks:
L0118 |    - Contract conformance tests (schema validation)
L0119 |    - Basic render smoke test
L0120 |    - Deterministic intent → animation mapping tests
L0121 | 
L0122 | ## Open Questions (Tracked, Not Blocking)
L0123 | - Should Gizzi avatar be:
L0124 |   - a) Always-on sidebar presence, or
L0125 |   - b) A summonable capsule window (recommended: summonable to reduce GPU load)?
L0126 | - Do we prioritize:
L0127 |   - a) stylized minimal face (low uncanny risk), or
L0128 |   - b) higher realism with visemes (higher engineering + uncanny risk)?
L0129 |

##### Full Extract (verbatim with line numbers)
```text
L0001 | # A2rchitech Session Summary — ShellUI Brain Runtime + UI Integration + E2E QA
L0002 | 
L0003 | **Date:** 2026-01-26 (America/Chicago)  
L0004 | **Topic:** ShellUI agent-native chat UI on top of Brain Runtime sessions; integration + hardening; E2E QA prompt.
L0005 | 
L0006 | ---
L0007 | 
L0008 | ## 1) Product Direction Decision: Fork vs Rebuild
L0009 | 
L0010 | - Initial idea: fork an OSS chat interface (e.g., LobeChat vs Open WebUI) and wire its functionality into A2rchitech.
L0011 | - Conclusion: **avoid forking for core product** if proprietary branding + long-term maintainability matter.
L0012 |   - Use OSS projects as **reference implementations** (UX patterns + feature checklist), but implement capabilities in A2rchitech’s own **Brain Session** contract.
L0013 | - Reason: reduce licensing/branding constraints, avoid inheriting framework coupling, and keep a stable internal API boundary.
L0014 | 
L0015 | ---
L0016 | 
L0017 | ## 2) Core Architecture: “Everything is a Brain Session”
L0018 | 
L0019 | A unified **Brain Runtime** design where a “model” can be:
L0020 | 
L0021 | - **Native API adapter**: Claude/Gemini/OpenAI/Qwen, etc.
L0022 | - **Local runtime**: Ollama / vLLM / llama.cpp
L0023 | - **CLI agent process**: Claude Code, Gemini CLI, Qwen CLI wrapper, Codex CLI (if used)
L0024 | 
L0025 | ### Key principles
L0026 | - **Single frontend contract**: UI talks only to the Brain Gateway (Responses/OpenResponses-shaped streaming).
L0027 | - **Stateful sessions**: kernel moves from one-off calls to resumable, streaming sessions.
L0028 | - **Dual-stream UX**:
L0029 |   - Chat stream = narrative output
L0030 |   - Console drawer = raw terminal/log stream
L0031 | 
L0032 | ---
L0033 | 
L0034 | ## 3) Backend Capabilities Spec (Brain Gateway + Orchestrator + Router)
L0035 | 
L0036 | ### Brain Gateway (single contract to frontend)
L0037 | - `POST /v1/sessions`
L0038 | - `GET /v1/sessions/:id/events` (SSE)
L0039 | - `POST /v1/sessions/:id/input`
L0040 | - `GET /v1/brain/profiles`
L0041 | - `POST /v1/brain/route`
L0042 | 
L0043 | ### Session Orchestrator
L0044 | - Select runtime type (api/local/cli)
L0045 | - Spawn CLI under PTY with sandboxing + workspace dir
L0046 | - Create API clients + local runtime connectors
L0047 | - Normalize outputs into a single event stream
L0048 | 
L0049 | ### Model Router (policy engine)
L0050 | - Stores **Brain Profiles** (capabilities, cost, privacy)
L0051 | - Produces **Brain Plans**:
L0052 |   - primary selection
L0053 |   - fallbacks
L0054 |   - blockers (missing binary, missing key, missing model, missing deps)
L0055 | 
L0056 | ### Capability Registry (OpenRouter-like)
L0057 | Per brain profile:
L0058 | - `type: api|cli|local`
L0059 | - capability flags (tools, vision, long_context, code, json_mode, fast_stream)
L0060 | - requirements (api_key, binary_path, model_downloaded, deps)
L0061 | - install + auth recipes (deep links + scripts)
L0062 | - healthcheck
L0063 | 
L0064 | ---
L0065 | 
L0066 | ## 4) UX Flows Required
L0067 | 
L0068 | ### A) First open / onboarding (Brain Setup Wizard)
L0069 | - Detect OS + shell + permissions.
L0070 | - Offer three paths:
L0071 |   1) Cloud API brain (enter key + test)
L0072 |   2) Local brain (install runtime/model + test)
L0073 |   3) CLI brain (install deps + CLI + test)
L0074 | - Store default brain + fallback chain.
L0075 | 
L0076 | ### B) Homepage “Run an Agent”
L0077 | - Start brain session explicitly (CLI auto-opens console drawer).
L0078 | 
L0079 | ### C) Chat send with no agent selected
L0080 | - Do **not** stub response.
L0081 | - Backend emits `brain.required`.
L0082 | - UI shows “Choose Brain” card:
L0083 |   - Auto Router
L0084 |   - Connect API
L0085 |   - Install CLI
L0086 |   - Use Local
L0087 | - After selection: **replay the original message automatically**.
L0088 | 
L0089 | ---
L0090 | 
L0091 | ## 5) ShellUI UI Build: Required Frontend Features
L0092 | 
L0093 | ### Core wiring
L0094 | - Session-aware stores:
L0095 |   - `SessionStore` (SSE attach, dedupe, reconnect, lifecycle)
L0096 |   - `BrainStore` (profiles + route plans)
L0097 | - Message rendering as **parts**, not flat text:
L0098 |   - streaming text
L0099 |   - tool calls/results (collapsible)
L0100 |   - artifacts
L0101 |   - (optional) terminal preview blocks
L0102 | - Console drawer terminal:
L0103 |   - global mount
L0104 |   - subscribes to active session
L0105 |   - auto-open on first `terminal.delta` for CLI sessions
L0106 | 
L0107 | ### Expected UX polish
L0108 | - message states (sending/streaming/done/error)
L0109 | - retry/regenerate/stop
L0110 | - thread list features (rename/pin/search)
L0111 | - clear, non-raw error blocks
L0112 | - router transparency (capabilities + fallbacks + blockers + deep links)
L0113 | 
L0114 | ---
L0115 | 
L0116 | ## 6) Integration Bridge (No Rewrite)
L0117 | 
L0118 | Problem: new UI components existed but weren’t integrated into real ShellUI entry points.
L0119 | 
L0120 | Solution: **Adapter layer** approach:
L0121 | 
L0122 | - `ChatInterface.tsx` keeps legacy signature but renders new `ChatView`.
L0123 | - `ConsoleDrawer` becomes global and binds to `activeSessionId` via a tiny UI store.
L0124 | - `BrainContextAdapter.tsx` preserves old BrainContext interface while delegating to BrainStore.
L0125 | - `conversationSync.ts` keeps legacy ConversationStore previews and thread metadata updated from SessionStore events.
L0126 | - `ChatStorageAdapter.ts` bridges legacy persistence as needed without creating a second writer.
L0127 | 
L0128 | Key principle: **one source of truth** for message content; everything else derived.
L0129 | 
L0130 | ---
L0131 | 
L0132 | ## 7) Production Hardening Requirements
L0133 | 
L0134 | Hardening deliverables and proof expectations:
L0135 | 
L0136 | - `TRUTH_SOURCES.md`: single write owners per entity (threads/messages/status/transcripts).
L0137 | - `EVENT_MODEL.md`: dedupe strategy + boundary conditions + reconnect behavior.
L0138 | - `SHELL_UI_TREE.md`: real mounted entry + root layout + where Chat + Drawer live.
L0139 | - `CONSOLE_DRAWER_RULES.md`: CLI vs API behavior.
L0140 | - `SHELLUI_CHAT_VERIFY.md`: pass/fail test matrix.
L0141 | - SSE instrumentation: open/close counts, leak detection, duplicate drop stats.
L0142 | - `DiagnosticPanel.tsx`: runs verification matrix + exports artifacts.
L0143 | 
L0144 | “Death tests” emphasized:
L0145 | 1) thread-switch leak test (many switches; SSE count stable)
L0146 | 2) reconnect mid-stream (no dup, no double-complete)
L0147 | 3) CLI lifecycle (PTY terminates on close; no zombie sessions)
L0148 | 
L0149 | ---
L0150 | 
L0151 | ## 8) E2E Website QA: Agent Prompt Pattern
L0152 | 
L0153 | A deterministic e2e QA prompt was produced to make an agent:
L0154 | 
L0155 | - Inventory every page + widget
L0156 | - Enforce stable selectors (`data-testid`)
L0157 | - Create a test matrix mapping every inventory item → test ID
L0158 | - Implement Playwright tests (smoke + regression + a11y basics)
L0159 | - Run tests and export artifacts (HTML report, screenshots, videos, traces)
L0160 | - Produce defect report with repro steps + artifact paths
L0161 | - Fail build if inventory items lack tests, and fail on console errors/network failures (unless allowlisted)
L0162 | 
L0163 | ---
L0164 | 
L0165 | ## Notes
L0166 | 
L0167 | - A “prompt for the agent to look for something” was noted during the session, but it was not the primary focus of this summary; the primary work was defining and executing the ShellUI Brain Session UI architecture + integration + hardening, and producing the e2e QA agent prompt.
```

##### Full Extract (verbatim with line numbers)
```text
L0001 | ---
L0002 | topic: A2rchitech • Registry + Marketplace Control Plane + UI
L0003 | date: 2026-01-26
L0004 | timezone: America/Chicago
L0005 | command: "gizzi save a2r session"

L0009 |   - https://www.aitmpl.com/agents
L0010 | ---
L0011 | 
L0012 | # A2rchitech Session Summary — Registry + Marketplace
L0013 | 
L0014 | ## Context
L0015 | You’re building **A2rchitech** as a productivity OS / agentic platform. The focus of this session was the **capability marketplace** concept: how to discover/import **skills, tools, agents/personas, prompts, workflows, and packs** from external repos/marketplaces while keeping an **authoritative registry** inside the platform.
L0016 | 
L0017 | ## Core Decision
L0018 | **Registry and Marketplace are distinct control-plane concepts but should appear as one UI surface.**
L0019 | 
L0020 | - **Registry (authoritative system of record):** editable, testable, activatable.
L0021 | - **Marketplace (discovery/import lens):** browse/inspect/import → **draft** only (never direct activation).
L0022 | 
L0023 | Recommended UI: one “Registry” tab with two modes:
L0024 | - **Registered**
L0025 | - **Marketplace**
L0026 | 
L0027 | And a strict lifecycle boundary:
L0028 | **Marketplace Import → Draft (Available) → Registry Review/Test → Activate**
L0029 | 
L0030 | ## Architecture Model (Federated “Asset” System)
L0031 | Treat everything as a normalized **Asset** model with a single lifecycle. Asset types:
L0032 | - Skill
L0033 | - Tool (MCP/OpenAPI/CLI wrapper)
L0034 | - Agent/Persona
L0035 | - Prompt
L0036 | - Workflow
L0037 | - Pack (bundle)
L0038 | 
L0039 | Key platform layers:
L0040 | - **Source adapters** (read-only): GitHub, SkillsMP, MCP registries, prompt hubs, internal catalogs
L0041 | - **Resolver**: resolves an asset reference, pins to immutable versions, verifies, caches, returns a pinned asset record
L0042 | - **Content-addressed cache**: stores fetched assets by hash/digest for reproducibility + offline
L0043 | - **Policy/permissions**: capability-based permissions + trust tiers
L0044 | - **Sandbox runner**: executes assets under constrained permissions
L0045 | - **Audit/provenance**: logs of tool calls, provenance chain, activation history
L0046 | 
L0047 | Non-negotiables:
L0048 | 1) No execution without immutable pinning (SHA/digest).
L0049 | 2) Marketplace never activates; activation is Registry-only.
L0050 | 3) All imports go through the resolver and land as draft/available first.
L0051 | 
L0052 | ## UX Target (inspired by aitmpl)
L0053 | Reference experience: https://www.aitmpl.com/agents
L0054 | 
L0055 | Key UX pattern to emulate:
L0056 | - Browse across asset types
L0057 | - “Cart/Stack Builder” that builds a **pack** (bundled capabilities) and produces a single “import pack” action (optionally also a CLI command in your ecosystem)
L0058 | 
L0059 | Key governance difference to preserve:
L0060 | - aitmpl-like systems often do direct “install/enable”; A2rchitech must do **import-to-draft then promote**.
L0061 | 
L0062 | ## Prompts Produced (Agent Commands)
L0063 | ### A) Repo audit prompt (marketplace control plane)
L0064 | A full “repo cartography + gap analysis” prompt was created earlier in the session to force an agent to inventory marketplace primitives: schema, resolver, cache, sandbox, trust, audit, and UI.
L0065 | 
L0066 | ### B) Registry vs Marketplace decision
L0067 | Guidance: keep Registry and Marketplace separate as concepts, unified as one surface with two modes.
L0068 | 
L0069 | ### C) Agent prompt to build Marketplace UI correctly (A2rchitech)
L0070 | A detailed “UI implementation agent” prompt was created with:
L0071 | - hard rules (work only in a2rchitech, don’t implement backend unless needed)
L0072 | - Step 1: locate existing registry implementation
L0073 | - Step 2: add two-mode UI (Registered/Marketplace)
L0074 | - Step 3: UI-first data layer boundary (marketplaceClient + fixtures)
L0075 | - Step 4: quality bar (empty/loading/error states, badges, filters)
L0076 | - Step 5: acceptance tests
L0077 | 
L0078 | ### D) “Wrong directory” correction prompt (no git)
L0079 | A corrective protocol prompt was produced to force an agent to stop assuming repo scope, prove current directory using filesystem-only commands, locate a2rchitech, and re-run audits strictly inside it.
L0080 | 
L0081 | ## Artifacts Saved (Images / Visuals)
L0082 | ### Marketplace Tab Wireframe
L0083 | - **File:** `a2_marketplace_tab_wireframe.png`
L0084 | - **Purpose:** grayscale wireframe of the desired “Marketplace inside Registry” tab surface (Sources panel, filters, results list, details panel, provenance strip).
L0085 | - **Local artifact path:** `sandbox:/mnt/data/a2_marketplace_tab_wireframe.png`
L0086 | 
L0087 | ## Next Execution Steps (High-confidence)
L0088 | 1) In A2rchitech, locate the existing **Registry tab** implementation (routes/components/services).
L0089 | 2) Implement **two-mode UI** in that tab: Registered / Marketplace.
L0090 | 3) Marketplace mode:
L0091 |    - discovery UI + filters + detail panel
L0092 |    - Import action that creates **draft** registry entries (or calls an existing import/register endpoint)
L0093 | 4) Registry mode:
L0094 |    - keep authoritative edits/tests/activation tools
L0095 | 5) Add “Pack Builder” (cart) UX to mirror aitmpl’s usability, but route through draft + promotion lifecycle.
L0096 | 
L0097 | ---
L0098 | End.
```
## 3. Unified Intent (SOT Candidate)
### 3.1 Core user journeys (evidence-only)
- Brain setup wizard: detect OS/shell/permissions; offer Cloud API, Local brain, CLI brain; store default and fallback chain. (Evidence: `/Users/macbook/Desktop/a2r-sessions/a2r_session_2026-01-26_shellui_brain_runtime_ui_e2e.md` L68-L75.)
- “Run an Agent” flow starts a brain session; CLI auto-opens console drawer. (Evidence: `/Users/macbook/Desktop/a2r-sessions/a2r_session_2026-01-26_shellui_brain_runtime_ui_e2e.md` L76-L78.)
- Chat with no agent selected: backend emits `brain.required`, UI offers choices, and replays message after selection. (Evidence: `/Users/macbook/Desktop/a2r-sessions/a2r_session_2026-01-26_shellui_brain_runtime_ui_e2e.md` L79-L87.)
- Capsule OS interactions: move/drag/resize/snap/minimize/tabbing/reopen as real OS behaviors. (Evidence: `/Users/macbook/Desktop/a2r-sessions/A2R Session-ShellUI-Browsercapsule-openwork-cliorchestrator-uitars.md` L56-L66.)
- Marketplace import lifecycle: Marketplace Import → Draft → Registry Review/Test → Activate; marketplace never activates directly. (Evidence: `/Users/macbook/Desktop/a2r-sessions/a2r_session_marketplace_registry.md` L27-L29, L47-L50.)
- Proof-gated memory audit path: instrumentation and tests required to pass (proof, not promises). (Evidence: `/Users/macbook/Desktop/a2r-sessions/A2R_Session_Summary__Memory_v2_Proof_Gating_and_External_Memory_Systems.md` L60-L69, L127-L139.)

### 3.2 Core primitives (explicitly named in sessions)
- Agent Harness, Orchestration Kernel, Law Layer/Repo Law, Capsules & UI Runtime, Memory & Replay, Discovery over Search, Local-first, Marketplace. (Evidence: `/Users/macbook/Desktop/a2r-sessions/a2r_session_2026-01-26_external-signals_agent-harness_a2ui.md` L52-L61.)
- A2UI as schema-driven UI composition plane and AG-UI as agent-driven patch plane for UI/schema mutation. (Evidence: `/Users/macbook/Desktop/a2r-sessions/A2R Session-ShellUI-Browsercapsule-openwork-cliorchestrator-uitars.md` L31-L34.)
- Brain Session contract and Brain Gateway as the single frontend contract. (Evidence: `/Users/macbook/Desktop/a2r-sessions/a2r_session_2026-01-26_shellui_brain_runtime_ui_e2e.md` L17-L27.)
- Memory v2 primitives: truth-preserving writes, conflict resolution, decay/maintenance, graph memory, and RLM-influenced policies. (Evidence: `/Users/macbook/Desktop/a2r-sessions/A2R_Session_Summary__Memory_v2_Proof_Gating_and_External_Memory_Systems.md` L31-L56.)
- Asset model for marketplace/registry: Skill, Tool, Agent/Persona, Prompt, Workflow, Pack with resolver/policy/audit/sandbox layers. (Evidence: `/Users/macbook/Desktop/a2r-sessions/a2r_session_marketplace_registry.md` L30-L46.)
- Protocol stack: AG-UI (Agent↔User), MCP (Agent↔Tools), A2A (Agent↔Agent). (Evidence: `/Users/macbook/Desktop/a2r-sessions/A2R Session-AG-UI.md` L111-L119.)

### 3.3 Invariants (must always be true)
- Marketplace never activates; activation is Registry-only, and all imports land as Draft first. (Evidence: `/Users/macbook/Desktop/a2r-sessions/a2r_session_marketplace_registry.md` L21-L29, L47-L50.)
- No execution without immutable pinning (SHA/digest). (Evidence: `/Users/macbook/Desktop/a2r-sessions/a2r_session_marketplace_registry.md` L47-L50.)
- Proof-gating is mandatory for kernel/memory-critical systems. (Evidence: `/Users/macbook/Desktop/a2r-sessions/A2R_Session_Summary__Memory_v2_Proof_Gating_and_External_Memory_Systems.md` L127-L129.)
- OpenWork must be first-class in Shell UI (not an iframe). (Evidence: `/Users/macbook/Desktop/a2r-sessions/A2R Session-ShellUI-Browsercapsule-openwork-cliorchestrator-uitars.md` L20-L23.)
- AG-UI is not a UI toolkit (protocol only). (Evidence: `/Users/macbook/Desktop/a2r-sessions/A2R Session-AG-UI.md` L34.)
- UI-TARS is a tool layer, not a replacement for code-level agents. (Evidence: `/Users/macbook/Desktop/a2r-sessions/A2R Session-ShellUI-Browsercapsule-openwork-cliorchestrator-uitars.md` L26-L28.)

### 3.4 Non-goals (explicit or implied)
- Do not treat AG-UI as a UI toolkit; it is a protocol layer. (Evidence: `/Users/macbook/Desktop/a2r-sessions/A2R Session-AG-UI.md` L34.)
- Do not replace code-level agents with UI-TARS; UI-TARS is a computer-use tool layer only. (Evidence: `/Users/macbook/Desktop/a2r-sessions/A2R Session-ShellUI-Browsercapsule-openwork-cliorchestrator-uitars.md` L26-L28.)
- Marketplace must not activate assets directly (activation is Registry-only). (Evidence: `/Users/macbook/Desktop/a2r-sessions/a2r_session_marketplace_registry.md` L21-L29, L47-L50.)

### 3.5 Candidate SOTs (if conflicts must be preserved)
- Candidate SOT-A (kernel-first/CLI-first agent OS): “Kernel → Brain runtime → PTY subprocess → Event bus → UI projections” and “Kernel-first beats GUI-first for scale.” (Evidence: `/Users/macbook/Desktop/a2r-sessions/A2R Session-Brain CLI-ShellUI.md` L664-L672.)
- Candidate SOT-B (GUI-first swarm controller emphasis): “A GUI-first coding swarm controller.” (Evidence: `/Users/macbook/Desktop/a2r-sessions/A2R Session-Brain CLI-ShellUI.md` L634.)
- Candidate SOT-C (fork vs rebuild baseline): avoid forking OSS for core product vs Open WebUI fork baseline. (Evidence: `/Users/macbook/Desktop/a2r-sessions/a2r_session_2026-01-26_shellui_brain_runtime_ui_e2e.md` L10-L12; `/Users/macbook/Desktop/a2r-sessions/A2R_Session_Summary_2026-01-26_browser_os_cli_capsules.md` L250-L254.)

| Modern enterprises and tech enthusiasts alike are seeking an all-in-one platform that aggregates diverse technologies into a single ecosystem. Imagine a platform where you can update your household robot’s software, install new AI-driven skills to an industrial arm, or chain multiple AI services together – all through one intuitive interface. The goal is to combine technologies that are each robust on their own (advanced robots, powerful AI models, cloud and edge computing) in a way that multiplies their capabilities when working in concert. This synergy is comparable to the concept of the “Smart Factory” in manufacturing, where AI, IoT, and robotics converge to create intelligent, responsive systems ￼ ￼. But here the vision extends beyond the factory floor to every context: home robotics, enterprise automation, and beyond. | Framing/A2R Session- Ai Software company blue print.md [L0007 UNHEADED] "Modern enterprises and tech enthusiasts alike are seeking an all-in-one platform that aggregates diverse technologies in..." | /Users/macbook/Desktop/a2r-sessions/A2RCHITECH_CODEBASE_ATLAS.md [L0022 Repo Root: Top-Level Directories and Purpose] - `.codex/`: local Codex skill bundles and datasets. (.codex/skills/ui-ux-pro-max/SKILL.md) | Partial | crates/skills/src/lib.rs | UNSTATED | Modern enterprises and tech enthusiasts alike are seeking an all-in-one platform that aggregates diverse technologies into a single ecosystem. Imagine a platform where you can update your household robot’s software, install new AI-driven skills to an industrial arm, or chain multiple AI services together – all through one intuitive interface. The goal is to combine technologies that are each robust on their own (advanced robots, powerful AI models, cloud and edge computing) in a way that multiplies their capabilities when working in concert. This synergy is comparable to the concept of the “Smart Factory” in manufacturing, where AI, IoT, and robotics converge to create intelligent, responsive systems ￼ ￼. But here the vision extends beyond the factory floor to every context: home robotics, enterprise automation, and beyond. |
| To build this comprehensive platform, we must consider all the major components and stakeholders that will make it viable and valuable: | Framing/A2R Session- Ai Software company blue print.md [L0013 UNHEADED] "To build this comprehensive platform, we must consider all the major components and stakeholders that will make it viabl..." | /Users/macbook/Desktop/a2r-sessions/A2RCHITECH_CODEBASE_ATLAS.md [NO ANCHOR FOUND] | Missing | UNSTATED | UNSTATED | To build this comprehensive platform, we must consider all the major components and stakeholders that will make it viable and valuable: |
| For technology developers and vendors (robotics manufacturers, software providers, AI model creators), the unified platform should be friendly and open to integration. They should be able to plug in their products via well-defined APIs and standards, rather than reinventing the wheel for connectivity. An example of this philosophy in action is the emerging robot-agnostic operating systems like Wandelbots NOVA, which connect entire robotics landscapes via open, robust interfaces – allowing new sensors, legacy hardware, or different brands of robots to all hook into one system ￼. Such a platform provides native SDKs (e.g. in common languages like Python or TypeScript) so developers can use familiar tools to control and extend the system ￼. | Framing/A2R Session- Ai Software company blue print.md [L0017 UNHEADED] "For technology developers and vendors (robotics manufacturers, software providers, AI model creators), the unified platf..." | /Users/macbook/Desktop/a2r-sessions/A2RCHITECH_CODEBASE_ATLAS.md [NO ANCHOR FOUND] | Missing | UNSTATED | UNSTATED | For technology developers and vendors (robotics manufacturers, software providers, AI model creators), the unified platform should be friendly and open to integration. They should be able to plug in their products via well-defined APIs and standards, rather than reinventing the wheel for connectivity. An example of this philosophy in action is the emerging robot-agnostic operating systems like Wandelbots NOVA, which connect entire robotics landscapes via open, robust interfaces – allowing new sensors, legacy hardware, or different brands of robots to all hook into one system ￼. Such a platform provides native SDKs (e.g. in common languages like Python or TypeScript) so developers can use familiar tools to control and extend the system ￼. |
| Critically, to become a true standard, the platform should be vendor-neutral and open-standard (possibly even open-source at its core). This encourages broad adoption. Industry consortia and standards bodies can help here – for example, the OPC Foundation recently introduced a new OPC UA Companion Specification for Identification and Localization that defines a unified data model for spatial information across machines and robots ￼. This kind of standard is foundational for different vendors’ robots to “speak the same language” about location, enabling coordinated interaction in flexible production environments ￼. Incorporating and building upon such standards will reassure vendors that the platform isn’t a closed garden, but rather an inclusive ecosystem aligning with industry-wide norms. | Framing/A2R Session- Ai Software company blue print.md [L0021 UNHEADED] "Critically, to become a true standard, the platform should be vendor-neutral and open-standard (possibly even open-sourc..." | /Users/macbook/Desktop/a2r-sessions/A2RCHITECH_CODEBASE_ATLAS.md [NO ANCHOR FOUND] | Missing | UNSTATED | UNSTATED | Critically, to become a true standard, the platform should be vendor-neutral and open-standard (possibly even open-source at its core). This encourages broad adoption. Industry consortia and standards bodies can help here – for example, the OPC Foundation recently introduced a new OPC UA Companion Specification for Identification and Localization that defines a unified data model for spatial information across machines and robots ￼. This kind of standard is foundational for different vendors’ robots to “speak the same language” about location, enabling coordinated interaction in flexible production environments ￼. Incorporating and building upon such standards will reassure vendors that the platform isn’t a closed garden, but rather an inclusive ecosystem aligning with industry-wide norms. |
| While developers care about openness, consumers and end-users care about simplicity and reliability. For widespread adoption, the unified platform must abstract away complexity and offer easy, possibly one-click setups and GUI-based management tools. | For consumers at home or enterprise users, the platform should offer “app store”-like simplicity. Think of installing a new capability to a robot as easily as installing an app on your smartphone. The unified platform could host a marketplace of AI skills, drivers, and modules – vetted for compatibility – that users can enable with a click. In fact, platforms like Wandelbots NOVA advertise an integrated App Store for robotics applications, where you can develop once and deploy your robot app across different hardware via containerized modules ￼. | Any platform aiming to be a global standard for robotics integration must accommodate the full diversity of robotics hardware in the market. This includes industrial arms, mobile robots, drones, service robots, IoT sensors, and more – coming from established players in North America, Europe, and the rapidly growing cohort of Chinese robotics companies. In fact, China has become the world’s largest robotics market by far, and its domestic robot makers (like SIASUN, Estun, DJI, Unitree, etc.) are now highly competitive. In 2024, China accounted for 54% of new industrial robot installations worldwide – a record 295,000 units in one year ￼. For the first time, Chinese manufacturers sold more robots in China than foreign suppliers did, seizing a 57% domestic market share (up from 28% a decade earlier) ￼. Moreover, China’s robotics industry revenue more than doubled from 2020 to 2024, and Chinese brands’ market share in-country jumped to 58.5% ￼ ￼. These trends indicate that any “unified platform” must be truly international and not biased to a single region’s hardware or protocols. | Framing/A2R Session- Ai Software company blue print.md [L0045 UNHEADED] "Any platform aiming to be a global standard for robotics integration must accommodate the full diversity of robotics har..." | /Users/macbook/Desktop/a2r-sessions/A2RCHITECH_CODEBASE_ATLAS.md [NO ANCHOR FOUND] | Missing | UNSTATED | UNSTATED | Any platform aiming to be a global standard for robotics integration must accommodate the full diversity of robotics hardware in the market. This includes industrial arms, mobile robots, drones, service robots, IoT sensors, and more – coming from established players in North America, Europe, and the rapidly growing cohort of Chinese robotics companies. In fact, China has become the world’s largest robotics market by far, and its domestic robot makers (like SIASUN, Estun, DJI, Unitree, etc.) are now highly competitive. In 2024, China accounted for 54% of new industrial robot installations worldwide – a record 295,000 units in one year ￼. For the first time, Chinese manufacturers sold more robots in China than foreign suppliers did, seizing a 57% domestic market share (up from 28% a decade earlier) ￼. Moreover, China’s robotics industry revenue more than doubled from 2020 to 2024, and Chinese brands’ market share in-country jumped to 58.5% ￼ ￼. These trends indicate that any “unified platform” must be truly international and not biased to a single region’s hardware or protocols. |
| From a technical standpoint, this means the platform should be hardware-agnostic and modular in design. A robot arm from Company A and an autonomous vehicle from Company B should both be able to register with the platform and interoperate. Again, looking at real examples: Wandelbots Nova OS positions itself as “one operating system for your entire robotics landscape” across brands, enabling integration whether you “retrofit legacy hardware or scale across vendors” via a vendor-agnostic approach ￼. Similarly, ROS 2 (Robot Operating System, v2) has become a common middleware standard that many robots speak; Karelics Brain leverages ROS 2 so that it can easily support various sensors and devices through standardized drivers, eliminating the complexity of managing unique software for each component ￼ ￼. Our unified platform would likely build upon such middleware and extend it with cloud connectivity and AI integration. | Framing/A2R Session- Ai Software company blue print.md [L0047 UNHEADED] "From a technical standpoint, this means the platform should be hardware-agnostic and modular in design. A robot arm from Company A and an autonomous vehicle from Company B should both be able to register with the platform and interoperate. Again, looking at real examples: Wandelbots Nova OS positions itself as “one operating system for your entire robotics landscape” across brands, enabling integration whether you “retrofit legacy hardware or scale across vendors” via a vendor-agnostic approach ￼. Similarly, ROS 2 (Robot Operating System, v2) has become a common middleware standard that many robots speak; Karelics Brain leverages ROS 2 so that it can easily support various sensors and devices through standardized drivers, eliminating the complexity of managing unique software for each component ￼ ￼. Our unified platform would likely build upon such middleware and extend it with cloud connectivity and AI integration. |
| A concrete use-case of multi-vendor support is fleet management in heterogeneous environments. On a construction site, you might have robots from multiple manufacturers – drones, Boston Dynamics Spot-like quadrupeds, excavator robots, etc. The platform should provide a central interface to control all of them. In Karelics’ example, their cloud platform allows “seamlessly [controlling] robots from various manufacturers using a friendly, centralized interface” and sharing data like maps and task schedules among them ￼ ￼. We envision the unified platform doing the same, but on an even broader scale and with AI-driven coordination (e.g. an AI planner assigning tasks to the best robot for the job, regardless of make or model). | Framing/A2R Session- Ai Software company blue print.md [L0049 UNHEADED] "A concrete use-case of multi-vendor support is fleet management in heterogeneous environments. On a construction site, you might have robots from multiple manufacturers – drones, Boston Dynamics Spot-like quadrupeds, excavator robots, etc. The platform should provide a central interface to control all of them. In Karelics’ example, their cloud platform allows “seamlessly [controlling] robots from various manufacturers using a friendly, centralized interface” and sharing data like maps and task schedules among them ￼ ￼. We envision the unified platform doing the same, but on an even broader scale and with AI-driven coordination (e.g. an AI planner assigning tasks to the best robot for the job, regardless of make or model). |
| In summary, robotics manufacturers (whether in China or elsewhere) stand to gain because a unifying standard would enlarge their potential market (their machines could plug into any customer’s system if everyone speaks the same “language”). It also pushes them toward excellence in their niche (since the platform will make it easy to substitute components, each vendor must compete on performance and cost rather than locking customers into an ecosystem). This competitive yet collaborative environment accelerates progress – for instance, Chinese companies have rapidly improved hardware capabilities (like Estun’s heavy-duty arms and SIASUN’s fast welding robots) to meet rising demand ￼ ￼. A platform that can integrate those advanced machines with equally advanced AI guidance will truly unlock their potential. | Framing/A2R Session- Ai Software company blue print.md [L0051 UNHEADED] "In summary, robotics manufacturers (whether in China or elsewhere) stand to gain because a unifying standard would enlar..." | /Users/macbook/Desktop/a2r-sessions/A2RCHITECH_CODEBASE_ATLAS.md [NO ANCHOR FOUND] | Missing | UNSTATED | UNSTATED | In summary, robotics manufacturers (whether in China or elsewhere) stand to gain because a unifying standard would enlarge their potential market (their machines could plug into any customer’s system if everyone speaks the same “language”). It also pushes them toward excellence in their niche (since the platform will make it easy to substitute components, each vendor must compete on performance and cost rather than locking customers into an ecosystem). This competitive yet collaborative environment accelerates progress – for instance, Chinese companies have rapidly improved hardware capabilities (like Estun’s heavy-duty arms and SIASUN’s fast welding robots) to meet rising demand ￼ ￼. A platform that can integrate those advanced machines with equally advanced AI guidance will truly unlock their potential. |

Using standardized cloud APIs and having a flexible integration layer will be key. | While cloud computing is crucial, the platform must also embrace edge computing, especially for IoT and robotics tasks that require real-time performance or must run offline. Edge AI computing in IoT refers to running AI algorithms locally on devices like robots, drones, sensors, etc., rather than sending all data to the cloud. This is often necessary because robots deal with high-bandwidth sensor data and split-second decisions. A drone avoiding an obstacle, for example, cannot wait hundreds of milliseconds for a cloud round-trip; it needs to process the camera feed and react immediately on-board. By utilizing edge AI, “a drone can process video feeds locally for obstacle avoidance, reducing both latency and reliance on cloud connectivity” ￼. In general, edge computing reduces latency, saves bandwidth, and can improve privacy (sensitive data doesn’t have to leave the device). | Framing/A2R Session- Ai Software company blue print.md [L0065 UNHEADED] "While cloud computing is crucial, the platform must also embrace edge computing, especially for IoT and robotics tasks t..." | /Users/macbook/Desktop/a2r-sessions/A2RCHITECH_CODEBASE_ATLAS.md [NO ANCHOR FOUND] | Missing | UNSTATED | UNSTATED | While cloud computing is crucial, the platform must also embrace edge computing, especially for IoT and robotics tasks that require real-time performance or must run offline. Edge AI computing in IoT refers to running AI algorithms locally on devices like robots, drones, sensors, etc., rather than sending all data to the cloud. This is often necessary because robots deal with high-bandwidth sensor data and split-second decisions. A drone avoiding an obstacle, for example, cannot wait hundreds of milliseconds for a cloud round-trip; it needs to process the camera feed and react immediately on-board. By utilizing edge AI, “a drone can process video feeds locally for obstacle avoidance, reducing both latency and reliance on cloud connectivity” ￼. In general, edge computing reduces latency, saves bandwidth, and can improve privacy (sensitive data doesn’t have to leave the device). |
| Our unified platform should therefore support a distributed computing model: tasks are dynamically allocated either to the cloud or to edge devices depending on what’s optimal. Modern approaches like TinyML (tiny machine learning models optimized for low-power devices) and distributed federated learning can be incorporated so even small IoT sensors can participate intelligently ￼ ￼. For instance, a network of smart security cameras (IoT devices) could run lightweight person-detection models locally (to detect intruders in real time), while a central cloud AI aggregates their alerts and runs a heavier face recognition or decision-making model if needed. The platform would orchestrate these multi-tiered workloads transparently. | Framing/A2R Session- Ai Software company blue print.md [L0067 UNHEADED] "Our unified platform should therefore support a distributed computing model: tasks are dynamically allocated either to t..." | /Users/macbook/Desktop/a2r-sessions/A2RCHITECH_CODEBASE_ATLAS.md [NO ANCHOR FOUND] | Missing | UNSTATED | UNSTATED | Our unified platform should therefore support a distributed computing model: tasks are dynamically allocated either to the cloud or to edge devices depending on what’s optimal. Modern approaches like TinyML (tiny machine learning models optimized for low-power devices) and distributed federated learning can be incorporated so even small IoT sensors can participate intelligently ￼ ￼. For instance, a network of smart security cameras (IoT devices) could run lightweight person-detection models locally (to detect intruders in real time), while a central cloud AI aggregates their alerts and runs a heavier face recognition or decision-making model if needed. The platform would orchestrate these multi-tiered workloads transparently. |
| However, simply giving everything an IP isn’t a silver bullet – proper address planning and device management is needed to avoid chaos in large networks ￼ ￼. The platform should implement or integrate IoT device management practices: hierarchical addressing schemes (grouping devices by location or function) ￼, directories to keep track of devices, and security measures (for example, using IPv6 privacy extensions so devices don’t always broadcast the same identifier to the world ￼). In essence, IoT mapping refers to how devices are organized and reachable, and a well-designed addressing strategy (likely IPv6-based) ensures the platform can scale to millions of devices in a structured way. | Framing/A2R Session- Ai Software company blue print.md [L0071 UNHEADED] "However, simply giving everything an IP isn’t a silver bullet – proper address planning and device management is needed ..." | /Users/macbook/Desktop/a2r-sessions/A2RCHITECH_CODEBASE_ATLAS.md [NO ANCHOR FOUND] | Missing | UNSTATED | UNSTATED | However, simply giving everything an IP isn’t a silver bullet – proper address planning and device management is needed to avoid chaos in large networks ￼ ￼. The platform should implement or integrate IoT device management practices: hierarchical addressing schemes (grouping devices by location or function) ￼, directories to keep track of devices, and security measures (for example, using IPv6 privacy extensions so devices don’t always broadcast the same identifier to the world ￼). In essence, IoT mapping refers to how devices are organized and reachable, and a well-designed addressing strategy (likely IPv6-based) ensures the platform can scale to millions of devices in a structured way. |
| AI is a fast-moving field, and “mainstream models are getting better” at an extraordinary pace. A future-proof platform must be architected with adaptability in mind, so that new paradigms in AI can be integrated with minimal disruption. What might these new paradigms be? Some are already on the horizon or here: multimodal models that handle text, images, and actions together; advanced reasoning techniques like chain-of-thought prompting; and more specialized AI agents that can perform autonomous tasks. | Framing/A2R Session- Ai Software company blue print.md [L0077 UNHEADED] "AI is a fast-moving field, and “mainstream models are getting better” at an extraordinary pace. A future-proof platform ..." | /Users/macbook/Desktop/a2r-sessions/A2RCHITECH_CODEBASE_ATLAS.md [NO ANCHOR FOUND] | Missing | UNSTATED | UNSTATED | AI is a fast-moving field, and “mainstream models are getting better” at an extraordinary pace. A future-proof platform must be architected with adaptability in mind, so that new paradigms in AI can be integrated with minimal disruption. What might these new paradigms be? Some are already on the horizon or here: multimodal models that handle text, images, and actions together; advanced reasoning techniques like chain-of-thought prompting; and more specialized AI agents that can perform autonomous tasks. |
| For example, Google DeepMind’s recent work on Robotics Transformer (RT) models illustrates how AI paradigms are shifting to encompass robotics. Their RT-2 model is a vision-language-action network that can take web-trained knowledge and directly output actions for a robot ￼. In essence, “RT-2 removes [the] complexity and enables a single model to perform the complex reasoning seen in foundation models, but also output robot actions” ￼. This blurs the line between “thinking” (AI in the cloud) and “doing” (robot control) – one model can handle both. Our platform should be ready to exploit such breakthroughs: if a unified model can control a robot more intelligently, we should allow it as a module within the system. Likewise, if tomorrow’s GPT-6 or Claude Next can autonomously operate software tools (as AI agents), the platform could delegate higher-level decision-making to them while it handles the execution and safety layer. | Framing/A2R Session- Ai Software company blue print.md [L0079 UNHEADED] "For example, Google DeepMind’s recent work on Robotics Transformer (RT) models illustrates how AI paradigms are shifting..." | /Users/macbook/Desktop/a2r-sessions/A2RCHITECH_CODEBASE_ATLAS.md [NO ANCHOR FOUND] | Missing | UNSTATED | UNSTATED | For example, Google DeepMind’s recent work on Robotics Transformer (RT) models illustrates how AI paradigms are shifting to encompass robotics. Their RT-2 model is a vision-language-action network that can take web-trained knowledge and directly output actions for a robot ￼. In essence, “RT-2 removes [the] complexity and enables a single model to perform the complex reasoning seen in foundation models, but also output robot actions” ￼. This blurs the line between “thinking” (AI in the cloud) and “doing” (robot control) – one model can handle both. Our platform should be ready to exploit such breakthroughs: if a unified model can control a robot more intelligently, we should allow it as a module within the system. Likewise, if tomorrow’s GPT-6 or Claude Next can autonomously operate software tools (as AI agents), the platform could delegate higher-level decision-making to them while it handles the execution and safety layer. |
| Another paradigm is the rise of open-source and decentralized AI. We saw with DeepSeek-R1 that open models can rival closed ones ￼. This might mean hosting an “App Store” for AI models within the platform, where users can drop in a new model (much like plugins). | •	Modularity and Microservices: The platform should be built from modular components that can be independently updated or replaced. For example, the AI orchestration engine, the device communication layer, the user interface, etc., are separate services. This way, if a new technology comes along (say a new comms protocol or a new AI model type), that module can be added or swapped without rewriting the whole system. Cloud-native microservice architectures (with containers and orchestration like Kubernetes) are well-suited to this flexibility ￼. In fact, Wandelbots NOVA emphasizes its “cloud-native architecture, lightweight deployment, and Kubernetes-ready backend”, allowing it to run on-premises or in cloud and scale as needed ￼ ￼. | Framing/A2R Session- Ai Software company blue print.md [L0090 UNHEADED] "•	Modularity and Microservices: The platform should be built from modular components that can be independently updated o..." | /Users/macbook/Desktop/a2r-sessions/A2RCHITECH_CODEBASE_ATLAS.md [L0029 Repo Root: Top-Level Directories and Purpose] - `bin/`: helper scripts and orchestrators. (bin/dev-up, bin/dev-down, bin/platform_orchestrator.rs, bin/comfy-capsule) | Partial | crates/orchestration/workflows/src/lib.rs | UNSTATED | •	Modularity and Microservices: The platform should be built from modular components that can be independently updated or replaced. For example, the AI orchestration engine, the device communication layer, the user interface, etc., are separate services. This way, if a new technology comes along (say a new comms protocol or a new AI model type), that module can be added or swapped without rewriting the whole system. Cloud-native microservice architectures (with containers and orchestration like Kubernetes) are well-suited to this flexibility ￼. In fact, Wandelbots NOVA emphasizes its “cloud-native architecture, lightweight deployment, and Kubernetes-ready backend”, allowing it to run on-premises or in cloud and scale as needed ￼ ￼. |
| •	Open Interfaces and APIs: To encourage widespread adoption, all key interfaces should be open or standardized. This includes device interfaces (perhaps building on ROS2 for robot APIs, and standard IoT protocols like MQTT or OPC UA for sensor data), and AI model interfaces (using standard ML model serving protocols). Developers should be able to write extensions or custom integrations without reverse-engineering or special licenses. An open plugin system invites community contributions and third-party support, which accelerates the platform’s growth. We already see moves in industry toward open data models (e.g., the OPC UA spatial data model we cited, available free to all ￼). | Framing/A2R Session- Ai Software company blue print.md [L0091 UNHEADED] "•	Open Interfaces and APIs: To encourage widespread adoption, all key interfaces should be open or standardized. This includes device interfaces (perhaps building on ROS2 for robot APIs, and standard IoT protocols like MQTT or OPC UA for sensor data), and AI model interfaces (using standard ML model serving protocols). Developers should be able to write extensions or custom integrations without reverse-engineering or special licenses. An open plugin system invites community contributions and third-party support, which accelerates the platform’s growth. We already see moves in industry toward open data models (e.g., the OPC UA spatial data model we cited, available free to all ￼). |
| •	Security and Governance: Becoming a standard means users trust the platform. Robust security (end-to-end encryption, authentication for devices/users, fine-grained access control) must be baked in. Additionally, governance mechanisms (like certification for third-party apps/models to ensure they meet safety requirements) will be important, especially in domains like robotics where safety is critical. Additionally, governance mechanisms (like certification for third-party apps/models to ensure they meet safety requirements) will be important, especially in domains like robotics where safety is critical. | •	Continuous Evolution: A future-proof platform must not rest after version 1. It needs a roadmap for continuous improvement, informed by technological advances and user feedback. Regular updates (delivered over-the-air, as mentioned, ideally with minimal disruption) will keep it on the cutting edge. Cloud connectivity makes this easier – core logic can be updated in the cloud, and edge device firmware can receive OTA updates regularly (with rollback options for safety). The one-click updates already implemented by platforms like Karelics show how even complex robot software can be updated fleet-wide with minimal effort ￼. ### Intended capabilities from sessions (explicit evidence)
- Swarm orchestration as a first-class capability. (Evidence: `/Users/macbook/Desktop/a2r-sessions/A2R Session-AG-UI.md` L22-L26; `/Users/macbook/Desktop/a2r-sessions/A2R Session-Brain CLI-ShellUI.md` L634-L672.)
- Marketplace lifecycle with immutable pinning and draft→review→activate flow. (Evidence: `/Users/macbook/Desktop/a2r-sessions/a2r_session_marketplace_registry.md` L27-L50.)
- Proof-gated Memory v2 maintenance and verification harness. (Evidence: `/Users/macbook/Desktop/a2r-sessions/A2R_Session_Summary__Memory_v2_Proof_Gating_and_External_Memory_Systems.md` L60-L69, L127-L147.)
- Capsule OS windowing behaviors (drag/resize/snap/tab/restore). (Evidence: `/Users/macbook/Desktop/a2r-sessions/A2R Session-ShellUI-Browsercapsule-openwork-cliorchestrator-uitars.md` L56-L66.)
- OpenWork as a first-class Shell UI tab (not iframe). (Evidence: `/Users/macbook/Desktop/a2r-sessions/A2R Session-ShellUI-Browsercapsule-openwork-cliorchestrator-uitars.md` L20-L23.)
- AG-UI event-driven UI mutation as agent↔user protocol layer. (Evidence: `/Users/macbook/Desktop/a2r-sessions/A2R Session-AG-UI.md` L32-L59.)
